memos.markdown:
# Memory Goes to Wall Street: SK Hynix's $26.5B Nasdaq Landing

The week belonged to one number: **$26.5 billion**, the size of SK Hynix's Nasdaq ADR debut — the largest U.S. share sale ever by a foreign company. The stock closed its first day up roughly 13% at $168, a premium to its Korean line, and dragged the S&P 500 and Nasdaq higher with it. Underneath the fireworks sat a genuine thesis question, not just a liquidity event: the listing is a wager that AI-driven HBM demand has broken the memory sector's traditional boom-and-bust cycle. Whether investors are right will reprice the entire memory complex. Separately, Apple's trade-secret suit against OpenAI — alleging OpenAI recruited 400-plus Apple employees and solicited hardware prototypes — added the week's second dominant storyline, with real IP and partnership implications ahead of OpenAI's own IPO track.

## Deal activity

M&A skewed toward securing the semiconductor and AI-infrastructure supply chain rather than headline consolidation. The largest disclosed transaction was **Element Solutions' pending $14.5B acquisition of Solutics Advanced Materials**, a direct bet on specialty materials feeding the chip supply chain. **Micron's $500M minority investment in GlobalWafers** carried the same logic in smaller form — buying wafer-supply certainty rather than a target.

The AI-power buildout drove a second cluster. **EQT is acquiring Copia Power**, an integrated energy-and-digital-infrastructure platform, while **Meta is pursuing an undisclosed $6.5B cloud/AI capacity expansion** and a **$4B Ohio data center** (with reported Amazon involvement) sits on hold pending final terms. Value flowed to megawatts and materials, not to logos.

Portfolio pruning rounded out the tape: **e& is divesting its Vodafone stake for $5.95B**, and **NNS Holding is pursuing a stake in OCI** under Dutch takeover rules. The pattern across the week is defensive positioning — locking down inputs and power — rather than offensive scale grabs.

## Capital markets

Equity issuance was the week's real engine, and it was concentrated. SK Hynix's **$26.5B ADR** dominates every league table it touches and reopens cross-listing as a live tool for closing the "Korea discount." The 13% first-day pop and $168 close signal deep institutional appetite for AI-memory exposure specifically, not broad risk-on.

The IPO pipeline is filling behind it. **Gloo Holdings (GLOO)** filed its final 424B4 prospectus, and **OpenAI reinstated co-founder Greg Brockman** to lead key projects in a move widely read as IPO-readiness housekeeping — even as its litigation docket expands. On the venture-exit side, **General Fusion closed its de-SPAC with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III**, becoming the first publicly listed fusion company, a reminder that the SPAC channel remains open for long-horizon deep-tech that can't clear a conventional IPO.

Credit color was thinner and more cautionary. **TeraWulf fell 5% on a $3.5B debt raise** that markets tied to strain around its reported $19B Anthropic-linked commitment — a signal that the AI-infrastructure financing story has a leverage limit.

## What we're watching

First, the Element Solutions / Solutics ($14.5B) and e& / Vodafone ($5.95B) transactions are both pending and will define whether this week's supply-chain-and-divestiture pattern converts into closings or stalls.

Second, OpenAI's dual-track story — IPO preparation against a widening set of legal fronts (Apple's suit, the refiled Florida case, Musk's antitrust pivot against the OpenAI-Microsoft alliance) — bears watching for how litigation risk gets priced into any eventual filing.

Third, the Apple-CXMT DRAM rumor, if confirmed, would crack the Samsung/SK Hynix duopoly precisely as

# The Week the IPO Queue Became the Story

Three filings now sit in registration targeting a combined ~$3.5 trillion — SpaceX ($1.75T), Anthropic (~$965B), and OpenAI (~$852B) — an equity-supply test with no historical precedent. Against that backdrop, the week's announced M&A was modest in count but pointed in direction: chipmakers buying software.

## Deal activity

Qualcomm's ~$4B agreement for Modular is the week's signature transaction — the third case this cycle of silicon vendors acquiring the compiler/software layer rather than more silicon, aimed squarely at loosening the CUDA lock-in. Adobe's purchase of Topaz Labs (terms undisclosed) extends its pattern of buying model-adjacent imaging capability. Google/Wiz ($32B) remains in regulatory review with closing targeted for later this year.

## Capital markets

The convert market continues to reopen for AI infrastructure names: CoreWeave's 1.75% notes due 2031 traded above par this week, a meaningful signal after the winter selloff in neocloud credit. Quantinuum's June debut ($1.68B raised) continues to trade well, keeping the quantum window open. HBM supply reportedly sold out through 2027 shifts pricing power — and, eventually, capex financing needs — toward the memory complex.

## What we're watching

SpaceX float-size decisions, which will set allocation dynamics for the entire queue behind it; the Wiz closing timeline as the year's largest pending strategic deal; and TSMC's monthly sales print as the cleanest near-term read on AI accelerator demand.

Bottom line: supply of paper is about to test demand for the AI trade — and the acquirers who move before that repricing settle their currency questions first.

*(Example memo compiled from seed data as of Jul 6, 2026 — live memos begin with the first Friday run.)*
